title: The dynamics of leadership and success in software development teams
abstract: From science to industry, teamwork plays a crucial role in knowledge production and innovation. Most studies consider teams as static groups of individuals, thereby failing to capture how the micro-dynamics of collaborative processes and organizational changes determine team success. Here, we leverage fine-grained temporal data on software development teams from three software ecosystems - Rust, JavaScript, and Python - to gain insights into the dynamics of online collaborative projects. Our analysis reveals an uneven workload distribution in teams, with stronger heterogeneity correlated with higher success, and the early emergence of a lead developer carrying out the majority of work. Moreover, we find that a sizeable fraction of projects experience a change of lead developer, with such a transition being more likely in projects led by inexperienced users. Finally, we show that leadership change is associated with faster success growth. Our work contributes to a deeper understanding of the link between team evolution and success in collaborative processes.
summary_en: Open-source software projects are built by teams — but the teams change, and leadership within them isn't stable. This paper uses fine-grained commit data from three major software ecosystems (Rust, JavaScript, Python) to study how team dynamics determine project success. Key findings: workloads within teams are unevenly distributed, and stronger heterogeneity correlates with higher success; a single lead developer emerges early and does most of the work; a substantial fraction of projects experience lead-developer transitions, more often in projects led by less-experienced users. Leadership heterogeneity and instability are empirical facts that matter for innovation-policy design.
data_used: Temporal commit data on software development teams in Rust, JavaScript, and Python ecosystems, obtained from GitHub repositories. Covers thousands of projects and millions of commits over multiple years.
policy_relevance: For Hungarian digital-economy and innovation policy (NGM, NRDI Office): the paper supports innovation-support programs that fund individual lead developers rather than uniform team-level grants. Relevant to Hungarian open-source adoption strategy and IT-sector digital-commons funding. Caveat: the software-sector pattern may not generalise directly to other R&D-intensive activities with different team structures (e.g., experimental science labs).
